title: Changes to the pre-removal risk assessment for Myanmar - Canada.ca
content:
## Changes to the pre-removal risk assessment for Myanmar
Ottawa, May 7, 2021 —Individuals from Myanmar may now be eligible to apply for a pre-removal risk assessment (PRRA) if they received a final negative decision from the Immigration and Refugee Board of Canada or the Federal Court, or a final PRRA decision from Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC), on or between May 8, 2020, and May 7, 2021.
Before removing them from Canada, the Canada Border Services Agency will notify eligible individuals from Myanmar that they may apply for a PRRA.
Normally, an individual whose refugee claim or previous application for a PRRA was rejected, abandoned or withdrawn is generally not eligible to apply for a PRRA for at least 12 months after the claim was rejected.
However, the worsening conditions in Myanmar could put individuals in a risk situation, in which case they may warrant an additional assessment. For this reason, some individuals from Myanmar are now exempt from the 12-month bar on applying for a PRRA, depending on when they received a decision on their refugee claim or their application for protection.
Individuals from Myanmar who receive a final negative decision after May 7, 2021, are not eligible to apply for a PRRA for 12 months after the date the claim was rejected. Any recent changes in country conditions would have been considered when the refugee claim was decided or during the PRRA process.
It is important to note that the eligibility to apply for a PRRA does not guarantee its outcome. IRCC officers will continue to decide on each case individually, based on the information provided.
Individuals are responsible for keeping their PRRA application up to date and informing IRCC of any changes to their application.
In determining which countries to exempt, IRCC considers any recent event in a country that could place individuals in a risk situation similar to those defined in the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act (section 96—definition of a “Convention refugee” and section 97—definition of a “person in need of protection”).
